summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the applicant sought the respondent pay her costs – Where the respondent was wholly unsuccessful – Where the respondent had been deliberately dishonest, had failed to disclose a number of relevant matters and had failed to accept an offer which was better than the ultimate outcome at the final hearing – Where the respondent is ordered to pay costs related to the proceedings, as assessed.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HOGAN J:

On 25 October 2024, I made final orders to resolve the property settlement proceedings between the parties; I also published Reasons for Judgment in which I expressed the findings I had made and the reasoning which resulted in the orders which were made. The order also contained provisions to facilitate the making of any application for costs.
The respondent filed a Notice of Appeal in relation to the October 2024 orders; he subsequently applied for a stay of the operation of certain of the October 2024 orders pending the determination of his appeal; he later filed an Amended Notice of Appeal.
The parties ultimately resolved that the determination of the applicant’s application for costs would await the resolution of the respondent’s appeal.
On 20 December 2024, I made orders staying the operation of certain of the October 2024 orders; I also made provision for any party seeking the costs of and incidental to the application for stay pending appeal to take specified steps within 28 days of the finalisation of the appeal. On 30 April 2025, the Full Court of this Court dismissed the respondent’s appeal for the reasons delivered that day.
According to the Applicant’s 20 December 2024 Costs Notice, she had, by then, incurred professional fees and disbursements in the total amount of $938,735.54.

The competing positions

Broadly speaking, the applicant sought orders that the respondent pay her costs of the proceedings[1] on an indemnity basis or, alternatively, on a party and party basis; she sought that such costs include the costs of and incidental to specified interlocutory applications.[2] She relied on written submissions[3] and affidavits.[4] The affidavit of her solicitor, Ms Martin, contains, as annexures, a number of costs schedules which relate to specified interlocutory applications.
The respondent opposes the making of any order that he pay the applicant’s costs. He relied on written submissions[5] which were sealed before Ms Martin’s affidavit was sealed.
The consequence of the above is that the respondent has not had the opportunity to respond specifically to the content of the various costs’ schedules. Given the content of the written submissions filed on his behalf, I have proceeded on the basis that the respondent would oppose the applicant being granted leave to rely on Ms Martin’s affidavit and the costs schedules which are annexed to it and to any aspect of the applicant’s written submissions in reply which refer to the same on the basis that.
Given the orders which will be made, for the reasons expressed below, I do not intend to have regard to Ms Martin’s affidavit or those aspects of the applicant’s written submissions in reply which refer to it or the annexures to it.

Consideration of the applicable principles and the submissions made about them

The Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) makes it clear that, subject to specific provisions within it, each party to proceedings under it is to bear their own costs. The Act also makes it clear that, if the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, it may, subject to having regard to specified matters and legislative provisions, make any order for costs that it considers just.
Neither party was in receipt of legal aid.
The consequence of the October 2024 orders is that the applicant will own property valued at $9,703,228 (of which $2,988,636 is to be received by way of cash payment) and the respondent will own property valued at $14,554,842. The applicant will own real properties from which, if she chose, she could derive an income to supplement that which she receives from her casual employment; the respondent (either personally or via his control of various corporate entities and a discretionary trust) will continue to own real properties and various corporate entities through which he will continue to operate his business.
The reality is that each party’s financial circumstances are such that each can meet their own legal costs; the reality, also, is that the respondent has the financial capacity to meet an order for costs, whether calculated on the party and party basis or on an indemnity basis.
The respondent’s primary position at trial was that the court would not be persuaded that it was just and equitable in the circumstances to make any orders altering the parties’ interests in property. Given this position, it can easily be concluded that he was wholly unsuccessful.
The respondent was also unsuccessful in persuading the court to adopt his secondary position which was that the applicant should retain the property she then owned and receive a cash payment in whatever amount was necessary for her to receive property valued at 20 per cent of the total net value of the property of the parties.
Instead, the court concluded that the orders which are just and equitable in the circumstances of this case are orders which see the applicant receive property valued at 40 per cent of the total net value of the property of the parties. This was the position which the applicant advanced at the trial.
As well as being completely successful in advancing her position about the amount of property which it was just and equitable that she receive, the applicant was also completely successful in her quest to have specified real properties transferred to her.
The findings made about the respondent’s conduct in relation to the proceedings include the following:
(a) he completed and affirmed various Financial Statements that he relied on during the proceedings in a manner that deliberately obfuscated, rather than illuminated, his actual financial position;[6] and

(b) he failed to disclose relevant financial documents[7] and failed to provide information to the applicant about his decision to deal with property or the sale proceeds of the same, for example:

(i) he failed to disclose his June 2020 sale of the property at UU Street, Suburb VV (the UU Street property) in his Financial Statement filed in September 2020 in the proceedings;[8] and

(ii) he failed to make any reference in a Financial Statement affirmed on 15 September 2020 to the cheque for $624,643 (being the sale proceeds of the UU Street property) which his conveyancing solicitors had told him, prior to that, that they continued to hold on his behalf and which he allowed them to continue to hold until they couriered it to him on 15 December 2020;[9] and

(c) he deliberately attempted to hide the existence of the UU Street property sale proceeds by giving false evidence about the use to which such proceeds had been put – that is, despite knowing that his conveyancing solicitors held a cheque for $624,643 for him in their possession, he gave evidence in an affidavit affirmed on 15 September 2020 that the sale proceeds had been paid to the bank to reduce debt;[10] and

(d) he had deliberately been dishonest in giving evidence, had provided false or non-responsive content in some of his Financial Statements, had deliberately failed, on more than one occasion, to discharge his known obligation to disclose his disposition of property in various Financial Statements he affirmed and relied on in these proceedings;[11] and

(e) he failed to disclose the sale by C Pty Ltd (a legal entity fully owned and controlled by him) of real property at YY Street, Suburb ZZ in February 2022 and a Vehicle 1 for $420,000 in 2022;[12] and

(f) despite being aware of his obligation to make full and frank disclosure about relevant matters during these proceedings, he instructed solicitors who had acted for him in the sale of real property not to disclose a copy of the contract for the sale of that property to the applicant’s legal representatives;[13] and

(g) he systematically failed to disclose documents he should have disclosed to the applicant in compliance with the obligation of full and frank disclosure imposed on litigants in this jurisdiction;[14] and

(h) much of the content of various Financial Statements he affirmed in the course of the proceedings was deliberately false or provided with such disregard for its accuracy as to render them virtually useless in attempts to ascertain his true financial position at any time – the content of them was such that there was much force in the submission that the respondent had deliberately intended to preclude the court from being able to fully appreciate his true financial position;[15] and

(i) he decided not to provide the person appointed as the single expert witness to value the inventory of the business (and other property) with the keys to the motor vehicles when he attended the business’ caryard for the purpose of valuing the business inventory.[16]

Given the findings made about the respondent’s conduct generally, which were expressed in the October 2024 Reasons for Judgment (including those examples set out above), I am easily persuaded to accept the submissions made on the applicant’s behalf to the effect that the respondent’s conduct in relation to the proceedings was such that she was put to additional costs that she would not have had to incur if, at the very least, he had complied with the obligation of full and frank disclosure cast upon litigants in this jurisdiction.
I accept, by way of example only, that the respondent’s conduct and general approach in relation to the proceedings meant that the applicant had to issue more subpoenas than would otherwise have been the case – for example, to banks, financial institutions, third party financiers and property managers. I also accept, again by way of example only, that the respondent’s lack of candour, failure to comply with the obligation of full and frank disclosure cast upon him by the applicable Rules of this court and his determination to be deliberately dishonest and to obfuscate, rather than illuminate, information relevant to the proceedings in which the parties were engaged made it necessary for the applicant to bring a number of interlocutory applications,[17] including those by which she sought orders for disclosure, for leave to adduce adversarial expert evidence and for injunctive relief pending the delivery of Judgment.
I also accept that the manner in which the respondent approached compliance with interim orders requiring him to pay spousal maintenance to the applicant and meet other specified costs resulted in her incurring additional costs. Insofar as this issue is concerned, I note that the findings made about the respondent’s approach to complying with such interim orders included that:
(a) despite an interim order having been made on 31 August 2022, the respondent had not complied with it at the time he attempted to sell real property in 2023;[18] and

(b) the applicant had been required to file an Application for Enforcement (on 15 February 2023), which was disposed of by orders made on 5 October 2023;[19] and

(c) as a consequence of the respondent’s failure to pay the ordered spousal maintenance when it was due, the applicant filed a further Application in a Proceedings on 1 December 2023, by which she sought an order for the payment of the arrears.[20]

I note that the applicant succeeded on her application for leave to adduce evidence from experts other than the single expert witness appointed to value the real property of the parties and entities under the respondent’s control.[21] The consequence of her successful application was that there were insignificant increases in the values attributed to many of the real properties which were the subject of consideration in the proceedings.
I accept that, on 6 September 2024, the applicant offered to resolve the proceedings on the basis that she:
(a) retain all property in her possession, including partial property settlement payments that she had previously received; and

(b) receive real property situated at:

(i) 1 DD Street (and the contents of the same); and

(ii) 1 and 2 GG Street; and

(iii) 2 DD Street free of all encumbrances (with the respondent to also pay all outstanding rates and unpaid utility bills); and

(c) receive a payment of $1,750,000 from the respondent within 60 days.

This offer was made at a time when the parties were yet to receive the valuations of the corporate entities under the respondent’s control – entities which were ultimately valued at $8,289,391.
The October 2024 order saw the applicant retain all property in her possession (including partial property settlement payments that she had previously received) and receive real property situated at 1 DD Street (and the contents of the same), 1 and 2 GG Street and 2 DD Street free of all encumbrances (with the respondent to also pay all outstanding rates and unpaid utility bills) and receive a payment of $2,988,636 from the respondent within 45 days.
That is, the applicant received $1,238,636 more in cash than she would have obtained if the respondent had accepted her September 2024 offer.

Further discussion and disposition

Whilst others may disagree, I consider that the combination of:
(a) the fact that the respondent was wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings; and

(b) the respondent’s deliberate conduct in relation to the proceedings, as found in the October 2024 Reasons for Judgment (emphasised in part above); and

(c) the respondent’s injudicious failure to accept the applicant’s September 2024 offer (particularly in circumstances where, of the parties, he was the party with the best knowledge of the actual financial circumstances) to resolve the proceedings in a way that would have seen her receive $1,238,636 less in cash than she ultimately received,

persuade me to the opinion that there are circumstances that justify the making of an order that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the proceedings other than in relation to:

(a) the applicant’s unsuccessful attempt, by the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 6 June 2024, to seek an order that the chattels and motor vehicles then in the respondent’s possession be the subject of a further order for valuation; and

(b) the applicant’s unsuccessful opposition to the respondent’s application for a stay of the operation of certain aspects of the October 2024 order pending the finalisation of his appeal against the same.

In arriving at this decision, I reject the submission that the respondent will be subjected to what was described as a form of double-counting or a penalty. I do so because:
(a) the findings made about the respondent’s conduct in the resolution of the property settlement proceedings were made in the course of undertaking the necessary tasks of, amongst other things, making findings about the property of the parties and the value of the same and determining the financial circumstances of each of the parties; and

(b) whilst the respondent’s determination to approach the parties’ property settlement proceedings in the way that he did had consequences for the findings made in resolution of the same, the Act mandates that, in determining applications for costs, the court must have regard to a party’s conduct in relation to the proceedings in which they are involved.

Further, I do not accept the underlying proposition – at least as I appreciate it to be – that, in essence, a party whose deliberate decision to flout their obligation to make full and frank disclosure of relevant financial information and documents in a timely way during the course of proceedings (which conduct has the consequence of putting the other party to greater costs and which results in the court being required to do the best that it can in the obscured circumstances created by the deliberate failure to discharge the disclosure obligation) can avoid one of the potential consequences of that deliberate conduct – namely, an adverse costs order, including on the indemnity basis – by relying on their own failure to comply with provisions intended to ensure that litigation in this jurisdiction is fairly carried out.
The conclusion that the circumstances here justify the making of an order that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the proceedings does not, though, determine the basis on which such costs should be calculated.
Well-known authority makes it clear that, unless there are exceptional circumstances, an order for costs should be made on the party and party basis; I accept that to order costs to be paid on an indemnity basis is something which is a “very great departure” from the “normal standard” in this and other jurisdictions.[22]
In Colgate-Palmolive Company v Cussons Pty Limited,[23] Shepherd J provided some examples of circumstances that might justify the awarding of indemnity costs – usefully, Holden CJ in Munday v Bowman,[24] drew, from his Honour’s decision, the following:
(a) where it appears that an action has been commenced or continued in circumstances where a party properly advised should have known that he had no chance of success. In such cases the action must be presumed to have been commenced or continued for some ulterior motive or because of some wilful disregard of the known facts; and

(b) making allegations of fraud, knowing them to be false, and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud; and

(c) evidence of particular misconduct causing loss of time to the court and to other parties; and

(d) the making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions; and

(e) an imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise.
I also accept that the categories of circumstances which enliven the discretion to award indemnity costs are not closed.[25]
I consider that, given the circumstances of this case, the respondent proceeded to trial in circumstances where, properly advised, he should have known that he had no chance of successfully maintaining his position that the applicant’s application for property settlement orders should, for the reasons he advanced, be dismissed. I also consider that, given his knowledge of the parties’ financial circumstances, his failure to accept, within say, 24 hours, the applicant’s offer to resolve the proceedings (albeit made 10 days before the trial commenced) on a basis that would have seen her receive $1,238,636 less in cash than she ultimately received was unreasonable and imprudent.
I have given significant consideration to whether, given the applicable law, the circumstances in this case here persuade of the making of an order that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the entire proceedings (save in relation to a couple of specified applications) on the indemnity basis.
Absent the statutory starting point of each party bearing their own costs, I would have had no hesitation at all in making such an order.
However, and while others may disagree, I have ultimately determined that the order that is just in the circumstances is an order that will require the respondent to pay:
(a) the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the Application – Enforcement filed on 15 February 2023 (by which she sought enforcement of the respondent’s ordered obligation to pay her costs and a partial property settlement), with such costs to be calculated on the indemnity basis because the application was necessitated by the respondent’s failure to comply with a previous order; and

(b) the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed 3 June 2024 and the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 6 June 2024 (other than in relation to the issue of an order for the valuation of chattels and motor vehicles in the respondent’s possession), with such costs to be calculated on the party and party basis; and

(c) the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the proceedings (other than in relation to the Application in a Proceeding filed by the respondent and sealed 5 December 2024, by which he sought a stay of the operation of certain of the orders made on 25 October 2024), with such costs to be calculated on the party and party basis before 8 September 2024 and on the indemnity basis thereafter.

Whilst I acknowledge that requiring the parties to either reach agreement about the amount which the respondent will pay the applicant by way of costs or requiring them to undertake the costs assessment process provided by the applicable Rules of this court is likely to lead to further costs, I consider that such processes best ensures that both parties are accorded appropriate procedural fairness.
Whilst it may be thought unnecessary (and acknowledging that neither party has been heard about this issue), it also seems appropriate, in the hope of minimising further disagreement, that specific orders are made to provide a timetable by which the parties engage in the costs assessment process if they are unable to reach agreement about the quantum of costs to be paid by the respondent to the applicant.
